title: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
description: Find maximum sum subarray in circular array

examples.explanation: Array: [5, -3, 5], n=3. Start i=0: length=1: subarray [5], sum=5, max_sum=5. length=2: subarray [5, -3], sum=2, max_sum=5. length=3: subarray [5, -3, 5], sum=7, max_sum=7. i=1: length=1: [-3], sum=-3, max_sum=7. length=2: [-3, 5], sum=2, max_sum=7. length=3: [-3, 5, 5] (wrap: indices 1,2,0), sum=7, max_sum=7. i=2: length=1: [5], sum=5, max_sum=7. length=2: [5, 5] (wrap: indices 2,0), sum=10, max_sum=10. length=3: [5, 5, -3] (wrap: indices 2,0,1), sum=7, max_sum=10. Final max_sum=10.

Array: [-2, 4, -1, 4, -2], n=5. Start i=0: lengths 1-5: sums: -2, 2, 1, 5, 3; max_sum=5. i=1: sums: 4, 3, 7, 5, 3; max_sum=7. i=2: sums: -1, 3, 6, 4, 2; max_sum=7. i=3: sums: 4, 2, 0, -2, 0; max_sum=7. i=4: sums: -2, 2, 1, 5, 3; max_sum=7. Final max_sum=7.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays in the circular array by iterating through each starting index and extending to all possible lengths, wrapping around using modulo. Use this for small inputs or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
ladders.desc: Find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray in a circular array by checking all possible subarrays using modulo for wrapping.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the maximum sum circular subarray involves systematically examining every possible contiguous subarray in the circular array. Since the array is circular, subarrays can wrap around from the end to the beginning. We achieve this by considering each index as a starting point and extending to all possible lengths up to n (the array size). For each subarray, we compute the sum by iterating through its elements, using modulo arithmetic to handle the wrap-around when the index exceeds n-1. The maximum sum encountered is tracked and returned. Intuition: This method guarantees finding the optimal solution because it exhaustively checks all possibilities, making it straightforward but inefficient for large inputs. It's useful for understanding the problem structure and as a baseline for more optimized solutions. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize max_sum to negative infinity. 2. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1: a. For each length len from 1 to n: i. Initialize current_sum to 0. ii. For each position j from i to i+len-1: compute index = j % n, add arr[index] to current_sum. iii. Update max_sum if current_sum > max_sum. 3. Return max_sum. Time complexity: O(n³) due to triple nested loops (starting index, length, and elements in subarray), but careful analysis shows O(n²) because the innermost loop sums elements incrementally in practice. Space complexity: O(1) as only a few variables are used. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete solution by integrating the subarray summation from L1 into the full brute force framework. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle negative numbers by initializing max_sum to a very small value, incorrect modulo usage leading to index errors, or inefficient recomputation of sums. Real code pattern: def maxSubarraySumCircular(arr): n = len(arr); max_sum = float('-inf'); for i in range(n): for length in range(1, n+1): curr_sum = 0; for j in range(i, i+length): idx = j % n; curr_sum += arr[idx]; max_sum = max(max_sum, curr_sum); return max_sum.

examples.trace: Array: [5, -3, 5], n=3. Start i=0: length=1: subarray [5], sum=5, max_sum=5. length=2: subarray [5, -3], sum=2, max_sum=5. length=3: subarray [5, -3, 5], sum=7, max_sum=7. i=1: length=1: [-3], sum=-3, max_sum=7. length=2: [-3, 5], sum=2, max_sum=7. length=3: [-3, 5, 5] (wrap: indices 1,2,0), sum=7, max_sum=7. i=2: length=1: [5], sum=5, max_sum=7. length=2: [5, 5] (wrap: indices 2,0), sum=10, max_sum=10. length=3: [5, 5, -3] (wrap: indices 2,0,1), sum=7, max_sum=10. Final max_sum=10.

Array: [-2, 4, -1, 4, -2], n=5. Start i=0: lengths 1-5: sums: -2, 2, 1, 5, 3; max_sum=5. i=1: sums: 4, 3, 7, 5, 3; max_sum=7. i=2: sums: -1, 3, 6, 4, 2; max_sum=7. i=3: sums: 4, 2, 0, -2, 0; max_sum=7. i=4: sums: -2, 2, 1, 5, 3; max_sum=7. Final max_sum=7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Summing a Circular Subarray Given Start and Length
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of a subarray starting at a given index with a specified length, handling wrap-around using modulo.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for calculating the sum of a contiguous subarray in a circular array. Given a starting index and a length, it iterates through the subarray elements, using modulo arithmetic to wrap around when the index exceeds the array bounds. Intuition: In a circular array, a subarray can extend beyond the last element and continue from the first element. Modulo operation (index % n) ensures that we always access valid array indices by cycling back to the start. This allows us to treat the array as a circular buffer. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: array arr, start index i, length len, array size n. 2. Initialize sum = 0. 3. For j from i to i+len-1: a. Compute index = j % n. b. Add arr[index] to sum. 4. Return sum. Time complexity: O(len) for each call, as we iterate through each element in the subarray. In the full brute force, this is called O(n²) times, contributing to the overall O(n²) time. Space complexity: O(1), using only a few variables. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is used repeatedly in L0 to compute sums for all possible subarrays. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, incorrect modulo handling (e.g., using j % n without adjusting for start), or not initializing sum to 0. Real code pattern: def circular_subarray_sum(arr, start, length): n = len(arr); total = 0; for j in range(start, start+length): idx = j % n; total += arr[idx]; return total.

examples.trace: n=3, start=2, length=2. j=2: idx=2%3=2, arr[2]=5, total=5. j=3: idx=3%3=0, arr[0]=5, total=10. Return 10.

n=5, start=4, length=3. j=4: idx=4%5=4, arr[4]=-2, total=-2. j=5: idx=5%5=0, arr[0]=-2, total=-4. j=6: idx=6%5=1, arr[1]=4, total=0. Return 0? Wait, recalc: j=4: -2, total=-2; j=5: -2, total=-4; j=6: 4, total=0. Actually, output should be 0, but let's verify: subarray indices 4,0,1 gives [-2, -2, 4] sum=0. Yes, output=0. Correction: expected output 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over All Possible Subarrays in a Circular Array
ladders.desc: Generate all contiguous subarrays in a circular array by varying start index and length, using modulo to handle wrap-around.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the logic to enumerate every possible contiguous subarray in a circular array. Since the array is circular, subarrays can start at any index and have any length from 1 to n (the total number of elements). We use nested loops: the outer loop iterates over all starting indices from 0 to n-1, and the inner loop iterates over all possible lengths from 1 to n. For each combination, the subarray is defined by the start index and length, with elements accessed via modulo arithmetic to wrap around. Intuition: By considering all starts and all lengths, we cover every contiguous segment in the circular array, including those that cross the boundary. This exhaustive enumeration is key to the brute force method. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: array arr of size n. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. For len from 1 to n: i. The subarray is defined by start i and length len. ii. Elements are at indices (i + k) % n for k from 0 to len-1. 3. Process each subarray (e.g., compute sum in L1). Time complexity: O(n²) combinations, as there are n starts and n lengths. Space complexity: O(1) for the loops. Connection to L1: This logic provides the start and length parameters for the summation sub-routine in L1. Common mistakes: Missing length n (subarray covering all elements), incorrect loop ranges, or not handling the wrap-around in element access. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for length in range(1, n+1): # Process subarray starting at i with given length.

examples.trace: n=3. Start i=0: length=1: [5]; length=2: [5, -3]; length=3: [5, -3, 5]. i=1: length=1: [-3]; length=2: [-3, 5]; length=3: [-3, 5, 5] (wrap). i=2: length=1: [5]; length=2: [5, 5] (wrap); length=3: [5, 5, -3] (wrap). Total 9 subarrays.
n=2. i=0: length=1: [-2]; length=2: [-2, 4]. i=1: length=1: [4]; length=2: [4, -2] (wrap). Total 4 subarrays.

testCases.input: arr=[1,2,3], list subarrays for start=0, length=2
arr=[-1,-2,-3], list subarrays for start=2, length=3
arr=[3,-2,2,3], list subarrays for start=3, length=1
arr=[5,-3,5], list subarrays for start=1, length=2
arr=[-2,4,-1,4,-2], list subarrays for start=4, length=3
arr=[0,0,0], list subarrays for start=2, length=2
arr=[-10,5,5,-10], list subarrays for start=3, length=4
arr=[2,-1,2], list subarrays for start=1, length=3
arr=[100,-1,100], list subarrays for start=2, length=2

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Arithmetic for Circular Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Use modulo operation to access array elements in a circular manner, wrapping indices beyond the array bounds.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces modulo arithmetic as a technique to handle circular arrays. In a circular array, when an index exceeds the last valid index (n-1), it should wrap around to the beginning. The modulo operation (%) achieves this by computing the remainder when dividing by n, ensuring the result is always between 0 and n-1. Intuition: Think of the array as a circle; modulo acts like clock arithmetic, cycling indices back to 0 after n-1. This allows linear traversal to simulate circular behavior. Step-by-step: Given an index i and array size n, the circular index is i % n. For example, if n=5 and i=7, 7 % 5 = 2, so we access element at position 2. Time complexity: O(1) per operation. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in L2 to access elements when generating subarrays that wrap around. Common mistakes: Forgetting to use modulo, using integer division instead, or incorrect handling of negative indices (not needed here as indices start from 0). Real code pattern: circular_index = index % n.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Summing Elements in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of a contiguous sequence of elements in an array given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves calculating the total sum of elements in a subarray, defined by a start index and an end index (inclusive). It is a fundamental array manipulation task. Intuition: By iterating through each element from start to end and accumulating their values, we get the subarray sum. This is essential for many array problems, including maximum subarray calculations. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: array arr, start index start, end index end. 2. Initialize sum = 0. 3. For i from start to end (inclusive): a. Add arr[i] to sum. 4. Return sum. Time complexity: O(k) where k = end - start + 1 is the length of the subarray. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: In L3, modulo is added to handle circular arrays, but here we focus on linear summation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not initializing sum to 0, or using wrong indices. Real code pattern: def subarray_sum(arr, start, end): total = 0; for i in range(start, end+1): total += arr[i]; return total.

examples.trace: start=0, end=2. i=0: arr[0]=5, total=5. i=1: arr[1]=-3, total=2. i=2: arr[2]=5, total=7. Return 7.
start=1, end=3. i=1: arr[1]=4, total=4. i=2: arr[2]=-1, total=3. i=3: arr[3]=4, total=7. Return 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterating Through Array Elements
ladders.desc: Basic loop to traverse an array from start to end, accessing each element sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the simplest operation in array processing: iterating through all elements of an array using a loop. It is a prerequisite for any array algorithm. Intuition: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and a loop allows us to visit each element one by one, performing operations like summation or comparison. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: array arr of size n. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Access arr[i] for processing. 3. Example: print each element. Time complexity: O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity: O(1) if only using the loop variable. Connection to L4: This iteration is used in L4 to sum elements by looping through a subarray range. Common mistakes: Using wrong loop bounds (e.g., i <= n instead of i < n), not handling empty arrays, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): # Process arr[i].

approaches.name: Kadane's Algorithm with Wrap Handling O(n)
approaches.summary: Apply Kadane's algorithm to find the maximum subarray sum in the linear array, then handle the circular case by considering the total sum minus the minimum subarray sum. This works because the maximum circular sum can be either within the linear array or wrap around.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Circular Subarray using Kadane's Algorithm with Wrap Handling
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum sum subarray in a circular array using Kadane's algorithm and wrap-around handling.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray in a circular array where elements can wrap around from the end to the beginning. The key insight is that the maximum circular sum can occur in two scenarios: 1) As a regular maximum subarray sum within the linear array (no wrap-around), which can be found using Kadane's algorithm. 2) As a wrap-around subarray that includes elements from both ends of the array, which can be calculated as the total sum of the array minus the minimum subarray sum. The intuition behind the second case is that when the maximum sum wraps around, the remaining elements (which are not included in the maximum sum) form a minimum subarray sum. By subtracting this minimum from the total sum, we get the maximum wrap-around sum. The final answer is the maximum of these two values. However, there's an edge case: if all elements are negative, the maximum sum should be the maximum element itself, not the total sum minus minimum sum (which would be 0). The algorithm first computes the maximum subarray sum (max_kadane) using Kadane's algorithm, then computes the minimum subarray sum (min_kadane) using a modified Kadane's algorithm that tracks minimum instead of maximum. It also calculates the total sum of the array. The maximum circular sum is max(max_kadane, total_sum - min_kadane), unless all elements are negative, in which case it's just max_kadane. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array three times (or once with simultaneous tracking). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach efficiently handles the circular nature by leveraging the relationship between total sum and minimum subarray sum.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute max subarray sum using Kadane's algorithm. Initialize max_ending_here = 0, max_so_far = -inf. For element 5: max_ending_here = max(0+5, 5) = 5, max_so_far = max(-inf, 5) = 5. For -3: max_ending_here = max(5-3, -3) = 2, max_so_far = max(5, 2) = 5. For 5: max_ending_here = max(2+5, 5) = 7, max_so_far = max(5, 7) = 7. So max_kadane = 7. Step 2: Compute min subarray sum using modified Kadane's algorithm. Initialize min_ending_here = 0, min_so_far = inf. For 5: min_ending_here = min(0+5, 5) = 5, min_so_far = min(inf, 5) = 5. For -3: min_ending_here = min(5-3, -3) = -3, min_so_far = min(5, -3) = -3. For 5: min_ending_here = min(-3+5, 5) = 2, min_so_far = min(-3, 2) = -3. So min_kadane = -3. Step 3: Compute total sum = 5 + (-3) + 5 = 7. Step 4: Compute max circular sum = max(max_kadane, total_sum - min_kadane) = max(7, 7 - (-3)) = max(7, 10) = 10. Step 5: Check if all elements are negative? No, so answer is 10. The maximum circular subarray is [5, -3, 5] wrapping around to include both 5s, giving sum 5+5=10.

Step 1: Compute max subarray sum using Kadane's algorithm. Initialize max_ending_here = 0, max_so_far = -inf. For -3: max_ending_here = max(0-3, -3) = -3, max_so_far = max(-inf, -3) = -3. For -2: max_ending_here = max(-3-2, -2) = -2, max_so_far = max(-3, -2) = -2. For -1: max_ending_here = max(-2-1, -1) = -1, max_so_far = max(-2, -1) = -1. So max_kadane = -1. Step 2: Compute min subarray sum using modified Kadane's algorithm. Initialize min_ending_here = 0, min_so_far = inf. For -3: min_ending_here = min(0-3, -3) = -3, min_so_far = min(inf, -3) = -3. For -2: min_ending_here = min(-3-2, -2) = -5, min_so_far = min(-3, -5) = -5. For -1: min_ending_here = min(-5-1, -1) = -6, min_so_far = min(-5, -6) = -6. So min_kadane = -6. Step 3: Compute total sum = -3 + (-2) + (-1) = -6. Step 4: Compute max circular sum = max(max_kadane, total_sum - min_kadane) = max(-1, -6 - (-6)) = max(-1, 0) = 0. Step 5: Check if all elements are negative? Yes, all elements are negative. In this case, the maximum circular sum should be max_kadane = -1, not 0. So answer is -1. The maximum subarray is just the single element -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Kadane's Algorithm for Maximum and Minimum Subarray Sum
ladders.desc: Implement Kadane's algorithm to find both maximum and minimum subarray sums in a linear array.
ladders.explanation: Kadane's algorithm is a dynamic programming approach to find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray in O(n) time. The core idea is to iterate through the array and at each position, decide whether to extend the current subarray or start a new one from the current element. For maximum sum, we maintain two variables: max_ending_here (the maximum sum ending at the current position) and max_so_far (the overall maximum sum found so far). At each element, max_ending_here is updated as max(element, max_ending_here + element), meaning we either start a new subarray at this element or extend the previous one. Then max_so_far is updated as max(max_so_far, max_ending_here). For minimum sum, we use a similar approach but track minimum instead: min_ending_here = min(element, min_ending_here + element) and min_so_far = min(min_so_far, min_ending_here). The intuition is that by tracking both maximum and minimum, we can later use them to handle circular cases. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once for each calculation (or simultaneously in one pass). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This sub-routine is crucial for the full problem because the maximum circular sum depends on both the linear maximum sum and the wrap-around sum derived from the minimum sum.

examples.trace: Initialize max_ending_here = 0, max_so_far = -inf. Element 1: max_ending_here = max(1, 0+1) = 1, max_so_far = max(-inf, 1) = 1. Element -2: max_ending_here = max(-2, 1-2) = -1, max_so_far = max(1, -1) = 1. Element 3: max_ending_here = max(3, -1+3) = 3, max_so_far = max(1, 3) = 3. Element -2: max_ending_here = max(-2, 3-2) = 1, max_so_far = max(3, 1) = 3. So maximum subarray sum is 3 (from subarray [3]).

Initialize min_ending_here = 0, min_so_far = inf. Element 1: min_ending_here = min(1, 0+1) = 1, min_so_far = min(inf, 1) = 1. Element -2: min_ending_here = min(-2, 1-2) = -2, min_so_far = min(1, -2) = -2. Element 3: min_ending_here = min(3, -2+3) = 1, min_so_far = min(-2, 1) = -2. Element -2: min_ending_here = min(-2, 1-2) = -1, min_so_far = min(-2, -1) = -2. So minimum subarray sum is -2 (from subarray [-2]).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Wrap-Around with Total Sum and Minimum Subarray Sum
ladders.desc: The insight that maximum circular sum can be computed as total sum minus minimum subarray sum.
ladders.explanation: In a circular array, the maximum sum subarray can either be entirely within the linear array (no wrap-around) or it can wrap around from the end to the beginning. The key realization is that when the maximum sum wraps around, the elements not included in that maximum sum form a contiguous subarray in the linear array that has the minimum sum. This is because if you take the entire array and remove the wrap-around maximum subarray, what remains is a contiguous block (since the array is circular, removing a wrap-around segment leaves a linear segment). Therefore, the maximum wrap-around sum can be calculated as the total sum of the array minus the minimum subarray sum. Mathematically, if the array has total sum S and minimum subarray sum min_sum, then the maximum wrap-around sum is S - min_sum. However, this only holds if not all elements are negative; if all are negative, then S - min_sum would be 0 (since min_sum equals S), but the actual maximum should be the maximum element (which is negative). This logic connects to the full problem by providing a way to compute the circular maximum without explicitly checking all wrap-around possibilities, which would be O(n²). Instead, we use O(n) operations to find min_sum and S. The intuition is derived from the complement principle in circular structures.

examples.input: Array [5, -3, 5] with total sum 7 and minimum subarray sum -3
examples.trace: Total sum S = 5 + (-3) + 5 = 7. Minimum subarray sum min_sum = -3 (from subarray [-3]). Maximum wrap-around sum = S - min_sum = 7 - (-3) = 10. This represents the subarray that wraps around: take elements from both ends [5, 5] (skipping the -3 in the middle), which sums to 10. The intuition: the minimum subarray [-3] is what we exclude to get the maximum wrap-around.

examples.input: Array [1, -2, 3, -2] with total sum 0 and minimum subarray sum -2
examples.trace: Total sum S = 1 + (-2) + 3 + (-2) = 0. Minimum subarray sum min_sum = -2 (from subarray [-2]). Maximum wrap-around sum = S - min_sum = 0 - (-2) = 2. This could represent a wrap-around subarray like [3, -2, 1] (if arranged circularly), but note that the linear maximum is 3, which is higher. So in this case, the linear maximum is better.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Total Sum of an Array
ladders.desc: Calculate the sum of all elements in an array, a fundamental operation for the wrap-around logic.
ladders.explanation: The total sum of an array is simply the addition of all its elements. This is a basic arithmetic operation but is crucial for the circular subarray problem because it feeds into the wrap-around calculation. To compute the total sum, iterate through the array and accumulate each element into a running total. Start with sum = 0, then for each element in the array, add it to sum. After processing all elements, sum contains the total. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements, as we touch each element once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use one variable. In the context of the full problem, the total sum is used in the formula S - min_sum to find the maximum wrap-around sum. It's important to compute this accurately because errors here will propagate to the final result. A common mistake is to forget to initialize sum to 0 or to use the wrong data type (e.g., integer overflow for large sums). In Python, integers are arbitrary-precision, but in other languages, consider using long or similar types for large values.

examples.trace: Initialize sum = 0. Element 5: sum = 0 + 5 = 5. Element -3: sum = 5 + (-3) = 2. Element 5: sum = 2 + 5 = 7. Final total sum is 7.
Initialize sum = 0. Element 1: sum = 0 + 1 = 1. Element -2: sum = 1 + (-2) = -1. Element 3: sum = -1 + 3 = 2. Element -2: sum = 2 + (-2) = 0. Final total sum is 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array and Updating a Running Total
ladders.desc: Fundamental skill of traversing an array while maintaining a cumulative sum.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves looping through each element in an array and performing an addition to a running total. It's a cornerstone of many algorithms, including Kadane's algorithm and total sum computation. The process is straightforward: initialize a variable (e.g., total) to 0, then use a for-loop to go through each element from first to last. At each step, update total by adding the current element: total = total + element. After the loop, total holds the cumulative sum. The intuition is that by accumulating step-by-step, we avoid needing to store all elements separately for summation. Time complexity is O(n) as we perform constant work per element. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use one extra variable. This feeds into higher levels because both Kadane's algorithm and total sum calculation rely on this pattern. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop indices, not initializing the total correctly, or modifying the array unintentionally. In pseudocode, this looks like: total = 0; for element in array: total += element.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Process element 1: total = 0 + 1 = 1. Step 2: Process element 2: total = 1 + 2 = 3. Step 3: Process element 3: total = 3 + 3 = 6. Final total is 6.
Step 1: Initialize total = 0. Process element -1: total = 0 + (-1) = -1. Step 2: Process element 0: total = -1 + 0 = -1. Step 3: Process element 1: total = -1 + 1 = 0. Final total is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Introduction to arrays as data structures and performing simple addition.
ladders.explanation: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices. For example, an array [5, -3, 5] has three elements: index 0 is 5, index 1 is -3, index 2 is 5. Basic arithmetic involves operations like addition: adding numbers together, e.g., 5 + (-3) = 2. In programming, we often need to traverse arrays to perform calculations on their elements. The intuition is that arrays allow us to group related data, and arithmetic lets us combine values. This foundation is essential for the circular subarray problem because we manipulate array elements through addition to find sums. Time and space are not analyzed here as this is a conceptual level. This feeds into higher levels by providing the basic skills to handle array elements and compute sums, which are used in running totals and Kadane's algorithm. Common mistakes include confusing indices (starting from 0 vs 1) or mishandling negative numbers in addition. Real code pattern: in Python, arrays are lists, and addition is done with the + operator.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify elements: 1, 2, 3. Step 2: Perform addition: 1 + 2 = 3. Step 3: Add the next element: 3 + 3 = 6. So total sum is 6.
Step 1: Identify elements: -1, 0, 1. Step 2: Perform addition: -1 + 0 = -1. Step 3: Add the next element: -1 + 1 = 0. So total sum is 0.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum with Deque O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute prefix sums for the duplicated array (to simulate circularity) and use a deque to find the maximum subarray sum by maintaining a sliding window of valid indices. This approach is useful when you need to handle constraints like fixed window sizes or want to avoid the wrap-around trick.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Sum Circular Subarray using Prefix Sum with Deque
ladders.desc: Complete O(n) solution for finding maximum sum subarray in circular array using prefix sums and deque.
ladders.explanation: This solution handles the circular nature by duplicating the array and using prefix sums to efficiently compute subarray sums. The key insight is that for any subarray of length up to n in the circular array, we can find it in the duplicated array [A, A]. We compute prefix sums P where P[0]=0 and P[i]=sum of first i elements of duplicated array. For each ending index j, we need to find the minimum P[i] where i is in range [j-n, j-1] (window size n) to maximize P[j]-P[i]. A deque maintains candidate indices with increasing P values in the sliding window. We initialize result as max of all elements (handles single element case). We iterate j from 1 to 2n: 1) Remove from front if index < j-n (out of window). 2) Update result with P[j]-P[deque[0]] if deque not empty. 3) Remove from back while P[deque[-1]] >= P[j] (maintain increasing order). 4) Append j. Time O(n) as each index enters/exits deque once. Space O(n) for prefix array and deque. Connection to L1: This uses the deque maintenance subroutine to find minimum prefix sum in sliding window. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle all negative arrays (max is largest element), incorrect window bounds, not initializing result properly. Real code pattern: Initialize deque, prefix array, result=max(A). For j in 1..2n: while deque and deque[0] < j-n: pop left; if deque: result=max(result, P[j]-P[deque[0]]); while deque and P[deque[-1]] >= P[j]: pop right; append j. Return result.

examples.trace: Array: [5, -3, 5], n=3. Duplicated: [5, -3, 5, 5, -3, 5]. Prefix P: [0, 5, 2, 7, 12, 9, 14]. Initialize result=max(5,-3,5)=5, deque=[]. j=1: P[1]=5, deque empty, result stays 5, deque=[1]. j=2: P[2]=2, window start j-n=-1, deque[0]=1≥-1, result=max(5,2-5=-3)=5, while P[1]=5≥2: pop right, deque=[], deque=[2]. j=3: P[3]=7, window start 0, deque[0]=2≥0, result=max(5,7-2=5)=5, while P[2]=2≥7 false, deque=[2,3]. j=4: P[4]=12, window start 1, deque[0]=2≥1, result=max(5,12-2=10)=10, while P[3]=7≥12 false, deque=[2,3,4]. j=5: P[5]=9, window start 2, deque[0]=2≥2, result=max(10,9-2=7)=10, while P[4]=12≥9: pop right, deque=[2,3], while P[3]=7≥9 false, deque=[2,3,5]. j=6: P[6]=14, window start 3, deque[0]=2<3: pop left, deque=[3,5], result=max(10,14-7=7)=10, while P[5]=9≥14 false, deque=[3,5,6]. Final result=10 from subarray [5, -3, 5] (circular wrap).

Array: [-2, -3, -1], n=3. Duplicated: [-2, -3, -1, -2, -3, -1]. Prefix P: [0, -2, -5, -6, -8, -11, -12]. Initialize result=max(-2,-3,-1)=-1, deque=[]. j=1: P[1]=-2, deque empty, result stays -1, deque=[1]. j=2: P[2]=-5, window start -1, deque[0]=1≥-1, result=max(-1,-5-(-2)=-3)=-1, while P[1]=-2≥-5 false, deque=[1,2]. j=3: P[3]=-6, window start 0, deque[0]=1≥0, result=max(-1,-6-(-2)=-4)=-1, while P[2]=-5≥-6 false, deque=[1,2,3]. j=4: P[4]=-8, window start 1, deque[0]=1≥1, result=max(-1,-8-(-2)=-6)=-1, while P[3]=-6≥-8 false, deque=[1,2,3,4]. j=5: P[5]=-11, window start 2, deque[0]=1<2: pop left, deque=[2,3,4], result=max(-1,-11-(-5)=-6)=-1, while P[4]=-8≥-11 false, deque=[2,3,4,5]. j=6: P[6]=-12, window start 3, deque[0]=2<3: pop left, deque=[3,4,5], result=max(-1,-12-(-6)=-6)=-1, while P[5]=-11≥-12 false, deque=[3,4,5,6]. Final result=-1 (single element -1).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Minimum in Sliding Window using Deque
ladders.desc: Use deque to efficiently find minimum value in sliding window of fixed size.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine maintains a deque that stores indices of array elements in current window such that their values are increasing. For each new element at index j, we: 1) Remove from front if index is out of window (i < j-windowSize+1). 2) The front always has index of minimum value in current window. 3) Remove from back while value at back ≥ current value to maintain increasing order. 4) Append current index. Intuition: By keeping indices with increasing values, we ensure front has minimum. When new element comes, we remove larger elements from back because they won't be minimum as window moves. Time O(n) as each index enters/exits deque once. Space O(k) for deque. Connection to L0: In L0, we use this to find minimum prefix sum in window of size n. Common mistakes: Comparing values instead of indices for window bounds, not handling empty deque. Real code pattern: deque=collections.deque(); for j in range(len(arr)): while deque and deque[0] < j-windowSize+1: deque.popleft(); if deque: minVal = arr[deque[0]]; while deque and arr[deque[-1]] >= arr[j]: deque.pop(); deque.append(j).

examples.trace: arr=[3,1,4,2], k=3. Initialize deque=[], result=[]. j=0: arr[0]=3, deque empty, deque=[0], result=[] (window not full). j=1: arr[1]=1, window start j-k+1=-1, deque[0]=0≥-1, while arr[0]=3≥1: pop right, deque=[], deque=[1], result=[] (window size 2). j=2: arr[2]=4, window start 0, deque[0]=1≥0, result.append(arr[1]=1)=[1], while arr[1]=1≥4 false, deque=[1,2]. j=3: arr[3]=2, window start 1, deque[0]=1≥1, result.append(arr[1]=1)=[1,1], while arr[2]=4≥2: pop right, deque=[1], while arr[1]=1≥2 false, deque=[1,3], result.append(arr[1]=1)=[1,1,1]? Wait, at j=3, window indices [1,2,3], min is arr[1]=1, so result becomes [1,1,2]? Let's correct: At j=2, window indices [0,1,2], min is arr[1]=1, result=[1]. At j=3, window indices [1,2,3], min is min(1,4,2)=1, result=[1,1]. But output shows [1,1,2] which is wrong. Actually, for windowSize=3, we start output when j≥2. So: j=0: deque=[0]. j=1: deque=[1]. j=2: window [0,1,2], deque[0]=1 gives arr[1]=1, result=[1]. j=3: window [1,2,3], deque[0]=1 gives arr[1]=1, result=[1,1]. So output should be [1,1]. My example input was incorrect; let's use correct trace: For arr=[3,1,4,2], k=3, result minima for windows: [3,1,4]->1, [1,4,2]->1, so output [1,1]. Trace: j=0: deque=[0]. j=1: remove front? j-k+1=-1, deque[0]=0≥-1, while arr[0]=3≥1 pop, deque=[1]. j=2: j-k+1=0, deque[0]=1≥0, result.append(arr[1]=1)=[1], while arr[1]=1≥4 false, deque=[1,2]. j=3: j-k+1=1, deque[0]=1≥1, result.append(arr[1]=1)=[1,1], while arr[2]=4≥2 pop, deque=[1], while arr[1]=1≥2 false, deque=[1,3]. Final result [1,1].

arr=[5,3,2,4], k=2. Initialize deque=[], result=[]. j=0: arr[0]=5, deque=[0], result=[] (window size 1). j=1: arr[1]=3, j-k+1=0, deque[0]=0≥0, result.append(arr[0]=5)=[5], while arr[0]=5≥3 pop, deque=[], deque=[1]. j=2: arr[2]=2, j-k+1=1, deque[0]=1≥1, result.append(arr[1]=3)=[5,3], while arr[1]=3≥2 pop, deque=[], deque=[2]. j=3: arr[3]=4, j-k+1=2, deque[0]=2≥2, result.append(arr[2]=2)=[5,3,2], while arr[2]=2≥4 false, deque=[2,3]. Final result [5,3,2]? Wait, windows: [5,3]->min=3, [3,2]->2, [2,4]->2, so output [3,2,2]. My trace error: At j=1, window indices [0,1], min is min(5,3)=3, but deque[0]=0 gives arr[0]=5, which is wrong because we didn't maintain properly. Correct algorithm: At each j, after removing out-of-window from front, the front has min for window ending at j only if j≥k-1. So: j=0: deque=[0]. j=1: j-k+1=0, deque[0]=0≥0, if j≥k-1? 1≥1 true, result.append(arr[deque[0]]=5) but min should be 3. Problem: We need to remove from back while value≥current BEFORE checking front for min. So: j=0: deque=[0]. j=1: remove front? deque[0]=0≥0, so keep. Then while deque and arr[deque[-1]]≥arr[1]: arr[0]=5≥3 true, pop, deque=[]. Append 1, deque=[1]. Now j≥k-1 true, result.append(arr[1]=3)=[3]. j=2: j-k+1=1, deque[0]=1≥1, while arr[1]=3≥2 pop, deque=[], append 2, deque=[2], j≥1 true, result.append(arr[2]=2)=[3,2]. j=3: j-k+1=2, deque[0]=2≥2, while arr[2]=2≥4 false, append 3, deque=[2,3], j≥1 true, result.append(arr[2]=2)=[3,2,2]. Correct output [3,2,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix Sum for Circular Subarray Maximum
ladders.desc: Transform circular subarray problem to finding maximum difference of prefix sums in sliding window.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that any subarray in circular array of length up to n can be represented as a contiguous segment in the duplicated array [A, A] of length 2n. For subarray from index i to j in circular sense (wrapping allowed), if j≥i, it's normal subarray in original; if j<i, it corresponds to [i..n-1] + [0..j] in original, which is contiguous in duplicated array from i to j+n. We compute prefix sums P where P[0]=0, P[k]=sum of first k elements of duplicated array. Then sum of subarray from i to j (1-based in duplicated) is P[j]-P[i-1]. To find maximum circular subarray sum, we need max(P[j]-P[i]) for all j>i with j-i ≤ n (since subarray length ≤ n). This becomes: for each j, find min P[i] in window [j-n, j-1]. This is a sliding window minimum problem on prefix array. Intuition: By subtracting minimum prefix sum from current prefix sum, we get maximum sum ending at j. Time O(n) for prefix and deque. Space O(n). Connection to L1: We use deque to efficiently find that minimum. Common mistakes: Not handling all negative case, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: Compute duplicated array B = A + A; Compute prefix P[0]=0; for i in 1..2n: P[i]=P[i-1]+B[i-1]; Then use deque to find min P[i] in window size n for each j.

examples.output: Prefix sums and window minima concept
examples.trace: n=3. Duplicated B=[5,-3,5,5,-3,5]. Prefix P: index 0:0, 1:5, 2:2, 3:7, 4:12, 5:9, 6:14. We need max(P[j]-P[i]) for j-i≤3. For j=4 (P=12), window i in [4-3=1, 3], i values: 1(P=5),2(P=2),3(P=7). Min P is 2 at i=2, so sum=12-2=10. This corresponds to subarray from i=2 to j=4 in B: indices 1..3 in B are [-3,5,5] but actually in circular: i=2 in P means after 2 elements in B, which is end of first element? Let's clarify: P indices are cumulative sums over B. Subarray from a to b in B (0-based) has sum P[b+1]-P[a]. So for j=4 in P, it represents sum up to index 3 in B. We want i such that j-i≤3 means i≥j-3. For j=4, i≥1. So consider i=1: sum=P[4]-P[1]=12-5=7 (B[1..3]=[-3,5,5]). i=2: 12-2=10 (B[2..3]=[5,5]). i=3: 12-7=5 (B[3..3]=[5]). Max is 10. This matches circular subarray [5, -3, 5] (wrap).

n=4. B=[1,-2,3,-2,1,-2,3,-2]. P: 0,1,-1,2,0,1,-1,2,0. Need max(P[j]-P[i]) for j-i≤4. For example j=5 (P=1), window i in [5-4=1,4]: i=1(P=1):0, i=2(P=-1):2, i=3(P=2):-1, i=4(P=0):1. Min P is -1 at i=2, sum=1-(-1)=2. But actual max circular sum is 3 (subarray [3]). Let's compute properly: We need to consider all j from 1 to 8. j=3: P=2, i in [max(0,3-4=-1),2]: i=0(P=0):2, i=1(P=1):1, i=2(P=-1):3. Max sum 3 at i=2, which corresponds to subarray B[2..2]=[3] (since P[3]-P[2]=2-(-1)=3). So core logic works.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prefix Sum Array Computation
ladders.desc: Compute cumulative sums of array to enable fast range sum queries.
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum array P is built where P[0]=0 and P[i]=sum of first i elements of original array A (0-based). Formally, P[0]=0, P[i]=A[0]+A[1]+...+A[i-1] for i≥1. This allows computing sum of subarray from index l to r (inclusive) in O(1) as P[r+1]-P[l]. Intuition: By precomputing cumulative sums, we avoid repeated addition for each query. For circular problem, we duplicate array A to B of length 2n, then compute prefix for B. Time O(n) to compute, space O(n). Connection to L2: Prefix sums transform subarray sum problem into difference problem. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in indices, not initializing P[0]=0. Real code pattern: n=len(A); B=A+A; P=[0]*(2*n+1); for i in range(1,2*n+1): P[i]=P[i-1]+B[i-1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Duplication for Circular Simulation
ladders.desc: Create a new array by concatenating original array with itself.
ladders.explanation: To simulate circular behavior, we duplicate the array A of length n to form B = A + A of length 2n. This allows any subarray that wraps around in circular array to be represented as a contiguous segment in B. For example, if A=[1,2,3], circular subarray [3,1] corresponds to contiguous segment [3,1] in B=[1,2,3,1,2,3] starting at index 2. Intuition: By duplicating, we eliminate the wrap-around complexity and reduce problem to standard contiguous subarray on B with constraint that length ≤ n. Time O(n) to create B, space O(n). Connection to L3: This duplicated array is used to compute prefix sums. Common mistakes: Not handling empty array, inefficient duplication. Real code pattern: B = A + A or B = A * 2 in Python.

examples.trace: Original A has length 3. Duplicate by concatenating A with itself: B = [1,2,3] + [1,2,3] = [1,2,3,1,2,3]. Length is 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subarray Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute sum of contiguous segment of array using iteration.
ladders.explanation: A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements in an array. To compute its sum, iterate through the segment and add all elements. For array A of length n, subarray from index start to end (inclusive) has sum = A[start] + A[start+1] + ... + A[end]. Intuition: Summation is basic arithmetic; by iterating, we accumulate values. Time O(k) where k is subarray length, space O(1). Connection to L4: In duplicated array, we compute sums of contiguous segments to find maximum. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds, not initializing sum to 0. Real code pattern: sum=0; for i in range(start, end+1): sum += A[i]; return sum.

examples.trace: A=[1,2,3,4]. Subarray from index 1 to 3: elements [2,3,4]. sum=0; i=1: sum=0+2=2; i=2: sum=2+3=5; i=3: sum=5+4=9.
A=[-1,3,-2]. Subarray from index 0 to 2: all elements. sum=0; i=0: sum=0+(-1)=-1; i=1: sum=-1+3=2; i=2: sum=2+(-2)=0.
